walkthrough.title: Unfold the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Welcome to the desert planet Arrakis. Let's get Dune Imperium set up. First, unfold the main game board and place it in the center of the table. You will see a beautiful map divided into board spaces organized by faction and location. On the left side you will find the four faction influence tracks: Emperor, Spacing Guild, Bene Gesserit, and Fremen. On the right you will see the Arrakis desert locations and the combat track at the top. Take a moment to admire the artwork and then let's start filling this board.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Conflict Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the three conflict card decks. These are labeled with Roman numerals one, two, and three on their backs. Shuffle each deck separately. Stack them with Conflict one on top, then Conflict two in the middle, and Conflict three on the bottom. Place this combined deck face down on the designated conflict space on the board. Each round, you will flip one of these cards to see what reward is at stake in combat.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Imperium and Reserve Decks
walkthrough.text: Now find the large Imperium deck of cards. These are the cards you will buy throughout the game to improve your deck. Give them a good shuffle and place the deck face down on the board. Flip five cards face up into the Imperium Row. This is the market of cards available for purchase. Also find the smaller Reserve deck containing Arrakis Liaison and The Spice Must Flow cards. Place these face up in their Reserve spaces on the board. These are always available to buy.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Intrigue Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the intrigue cards. These have a distinct back with a dagger symbol. Shuffle them into a face-down deck and place it on the board in the intrigue space. Intrigue cards are powerful surprises that you will gain throughout the game. They come in three types: combat cards you play during conflicts, plot cards you play on your turn, and endgame cards that score bonus victory points. Do not look through them yet.

walkthrough.title: Place the Resource Tokens
walkthrough.text: Now let's set out all the resource tokens within reach of every player. You need three types: spice, which are the orange crystal tokens. Solari, which are the gold coin tokens. And water, which are the blue drop tokens. Place them all in separate supply piles near the board. These are the three currencies of Arrakis, and you will be spending them constantly.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Faction Tracks
walkthrough.text: Look at the four faction influence tracks on the left side of the board: Emperor, Spacing Guild, Bene Gesserit, and Fremen. Each track has four levels. Place the alliance tokens at the level two marker on each track. When a player reaches level two on a track, they claim that alliance token, which is worth one victory point. There are also bonus victory point tokens at level four on each track. Leave those in place for now.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Leader
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a leader card. These represent the great houses and factions of the Dune universe. Each leader has a unique ability and a signet ring icon that affects certain cards. For your first game, you can deal them randomly or let players choose. Place your leader card face up in front of you. Your leader's ability is active for the entire game, so read it carefully.

walkthrough.title: Build Your Starting Deck
walkthrough.text: Every player starts with an identical deck of ten cards. Find the ten starting cards. They have a unique card back that distinguishes them from the Imperium deck. Each player gets the same ten cards. Shuffle your ten cards and place them face down as your personal draw deck. Draw five cards into your hand. This is your starting hand for round one.

walkthrough.title: Take Your Agents and Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes two agent meeples in their player color. These are the workers you will send to board spaces each round. Place them near your leader card. You will also notice a third agent called the Swordmaster on the board. You do not start with this one. It costs eight solari to recruit during the game, but once you have it, you get a huge advantage with three agents per round. Also take a combat marker in your color and a score marker.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Combat Track and Troops
walkthrough.text: Place each player's combat marker at zero on the combat track. Now find the troop cubes in each player's color. Place them in the garrison on the board. Troops are what you deploy to the conflict area to fight. During the game, you will move troops from the garrison to the conflict zone, and the more troops you commit, the stronger you are in combat.

walkthrough.title: Place Victory Point Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player places their score marker on the zero space of the victory point track that runs around the edge of the board. The first player to reach ten victory points triggers the end of the game. Victory points come from winning conflicts, gaining faction influence, and certain card effects. Keep a close eye on this track throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Give Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player collects their starting resources: one water token. That is all you begin with besides your hand of cards. It does not sound like much, but your cards and leader ability will generate more resources quickly. Some leaders may give you additional starting resources or bonuses based on their unique abilities.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Mentat
walkthrough.text: Place the Mentat token on the board in its designated space. The Mentat is a special helper that any player can hire for two solari during their turn. When you hire the Mentat, you get to use it as an extra agent this round. It returns to the board at the end of the round for anyone to hire next round. Think of it as a temporary third worker.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Choose a starting player randomly and give them the first-player marker. Play goes clockwise from the first player. Unlike many games, the first player marker does not rotate automatically. Instead, there is a board space where you can send an agent to claim first player for the next round. Ready to fight for control of Arrakis? Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Round Overview
walkthrough.text: Dune Imperium is played over a series of rounds until someone reaches ten victory points or the Conflict three deck runs out. Each round follows five phases in order: reveal a conflict card, player turns, combat, makers, and recall. Let's walk through each phase so you know exactly what happens every round.

walkthrough.title: Phase One: Reveal Conflict Card
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, flip the top card of the conflict deck face up. This card shows what rewards are at stake for combat this round. The card lists three tiers of prizes. First place gets the best reward, which might include victory points, spice, troops, or influence. Second and third place get lesser rewards. Everyone can see the conflict card, so you know exactly what you are fighting over before you commit troops.

walkthrough.title: Phase Two: Player Turns
walkthrough.text: This is the heart of the game. Starting with the first player and going clockwise, each player takes one turn at a time. On your turn, you do one of two things: take an Agent Turn or take a Reveal Turn. You must take all your Agent Turns before your Reveal Turn. Once you take your Reveal Turn, you are done for this phase. Play continues around the table until everyone has taken their Reveal Turn.

walkthrough.title: Agent Turn: Play a Card
walkthrough.text: On an Agent Turn, you play one card from your hand face up in front of you. Look at the icons in the top left corner of the card. These icons tell you which board spaces this card can access. Then place one of your agents on a matching board space that is not already occupied by another agent. Each board space can only hold one agent per round, so if someone beats you to a space, it is blocked.

walkthrough.title: Resolving a Board Space
walkthrough.text: When you place your agent on a board space, you gain the benefits printed on that space. These might include gaining spice, solari, water, troops, influence with a faction, intrigue cards, or drawing extra cards. You also get to use the agent icons on the card you played, which provide additional bonuses printed at the bottom of the card. Some cards also have a trash ability that lets you remove the card from your deck permanently for a powerful one-time bonus.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Card Icons
walkthrough.text: Every card in your deck has icons that serve two very different purposes. During an Agent Turn, the icons in the top left corner determine which board spaces the card can access. But during your Reveal Turn, the card's persuasion value and combat strength at the bottom become important instead. This dual-use nature is one of the deepest parts of the game. A card that accesses a great board space might have weak combat stats, and vice versa. You are always making tradeoffs about how to use your cards.

walkthrough.title: Reveal Turn
walkthrough.text: When you have no more agents to place, or you choose to stop placing agents, you take your Reveal Turn. Flip all your remaining hand cards face up. These revealed cards contribute their persuasion and combat strength. Add up the sword icons on all your revealed cards. This is your base combat strength. Then add your deployed troops, with each troop worth two combat strength. Move your combat marker on the combat track to your total.

walkthrough.title: Spending Persuasion
walkthrough.text: During your Reveal Turn, add up all the persuasion icons on your revealed cards. This is your buying power. You can spend persuasion to acquire new cards from the Imperium Row or from the Reserve. Cards in the Imperium Row cost varying amounts of persuasion. The Reserve always has Arrakis Liaison for two persuasion and The Spice Must Flow for nine persuasion. Acquired cards go into your discard pile and will be shuffled into your deck later. Building a better deck is how you gain long-term power.

walkthrough.title: Deploying Troops
walkthrough.text: Several board spaces and card effects let you deploy troops from your garrison to the conflict area. Troops in the conflict area each add two points to your combat strength. You can also deploy troops during your Reveal Turn using certain card effects. The key decision is how many troops to commit. Every troop committed is powerful in combat, but troops that survive a conflict go back to your garrison, so they are reusable if you play smart.

walkthrough.title: Phase Three: Combat
walkthrough.text: After all players have taken their Reveal Turn, combat happens. Look at each player's position on the combat track. Everyone with at least one troop in the conflict area or at least one point of combat strength participates. Rank all participants from highest to lowest combat strength. The highest gets the first-place reward from the conflict card, second highest gets second place, and so on. Ties are broken by the player closest to the first-player marker going clockwise.

walkthrough.title: Intrigue Cards in Combat
walkthrough.text: Before combat resolves, there is a window where players can play combat intrigue cards. These cards can add extra combat strength, steal rewards, or cause other surprises. This is what makes combat in Dune Imperium exciting and unpredictable. You might think you are winning the conflict, but a well-timed combat intrigue card can swing the result. Always keep an eye on your opponents' intrigue card count.

walkthrough.title: Faction Influence
walkthrough.text: The four faction tracks on the board are crucial to winning. When you send an agent to a faction-aligned space, you gain one influence with that faction. Reaching level two on a track lets you claim that faction's alliance token, which is worth one victory point. But beware: if another player also reaches level two later, they steal the alliance token from you. You need to push to level four to lock in a permanent bonus victory point that cannot be taken away. Faction influence also unlocks powerful board spaces that require a minimum influence level.

walkthrough.title: The Spice Economy
walkthrough.text: Spice is the most valuable resource in the game, just like in the Dune universe. You primarily gain spice from the desert locations on the board, especially The Great Flat and Hagga Basin. Some spaces give you spice directly, while others let you harvest bonus spice that accumulates on the board each round it goes uncollected. Spice can be spent at certain board spaces or traded for other resources. Many of the most powerful cards in the Imperium deck also cost spice to use effectively.

walkthrough.title: The Makers Phase
walkthrough.text: After combat resolves, check the board for maker icons on spaces where agents were placed this round. If any desert spaces with the maker icon were used, bonus spice tokens are placed on specific board spaces. This means uncollected spice accumulates on the board round after round, making those spaces increasingly tempting. The longer spice sits unclaimed, the bigger the payout for whoever finally grabs it.

walkthrough.title: Recall Phase and Cleanup
walkthrough.text: At the end of the round, everyone picks up their agents from the board. All troops that were in the conflict area return to their owner's garrison. Discard all played and revealed cards from this round. Then draw five new cards from your deck. If your deck runs out, shuffle your discard pile to form a new deck. The conflict card from this round goes to the discard pile. Refill any empty slots in the Imperium Row from the Imperium deck. A new round begins.

walkthrough.title: Deck Building
walkthrough.text: Throughout the game, you are building and refining your personal deck. Every card you buy from the Imperium Row or Reserve goes into your discard pile and eventually gets shuffled into your draw deck. Better cards mean better turns. But there is a catch: your deck also gets bigger, which dilutes it. Trashing weak starting cards is just as important as buying powerful new ones. Look for cards and board spaces that let you remove cards from your deck. A lean deck draws your best cards more often.

walkthrough.title: Game End
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately when any player reaches ten victory points, or at the end of a round when the Conflict three deck runs out. If a player hits ten victory points during a round, you still finish that round's combat phase before the game ends. The player with the most victory points wins. If there is a tie, the tied player with the most spice wins. Victory points come from conflict rewards, faction alliance tokens, faction level four bonuses, certain Imperium cards, and endgame intrigue cards.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Desert
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first round of Dune Imperium! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three example players. Player one is playing Duke Leto Atreides, Player two is playing Baron Vladimir Harkonnen, and Player three is playing Glossu Rabban. Each player will take different actions so you can see how everything works. Follow along even if your player count is different.

walkthrough.title: Start of Round
walkthrough.text: First, flip the top conflict card. Let's say it shows a first-place reward of one victory point and two spice, second place gets one troop, and third place gets nothing. This tells everyone what is worth fighting over. Now each player looks at their five-card hand and starts planning.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Agent to Arrakeen
walkthrough.text: Player one plays a card with a city icon from their hand and sends an agent to Arrakeen. This space gives one troop deployment and one card draw. Player one moves a troop from their garrison to the conflict area and draws a card. A great first move because it builds combat presence while also improving the hand for later.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was Smart
walkthrough.text: Player one accomplished two things with one action. They deployed a troop for combat, which is worth two combat strength. And they drew an extra card, which means more options for their remaining Agent Turns and more cards to reveal for combat strength and persuasion later. Early troop deployment is important because it signals to opponents that you are serious about this round's conflict.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Agent to Wealth
walkthrough.text: Player two plays a card with an Emperor icon and sends an agent to the Wealth space. This gives two solari and one influence with the Emperor faction. Player two takes two gold solari tokens and moves their marker up one step on the Emperor influence track. Solari is the workhorse currency — you need it for the Swordmaster, the Mentat, and many card effects.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Agent to the Great Flat
walkthrough.text: Player three plays a card with a spice trade icon and sends an agent to The Great Flat. This space costs one water to use but rewards two spice plus any bonus spice that has accumulated there. Player three pays one water and collects the spice. Spice is the premium resource in this game, and Player three just made a big grab.

walkthrough.title: The Dual Nature of Cards
walkthrough.text: Here is the first big lesson for new players. Each card in your hand serves two completely different purposes, and you have to decide which purpose to use. When you play a card on an Agent Turn, you use its top-left icons to access a board space. But when you keep a card for your Reveal Turn, you use its combat strength and persuasion instead. You cannot do both with the same card. So every time you play a card for its agent icon, you are giving up its reveal value, and vice versa.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring the Swordmaster
walkthrough.text: All three players have been spending their solari on other things, and nobody has saved up for the Swordmaster. The Swordmaster costs eight solari and gives you a permanent third agent for the rest of the game. That means one extra board space every single round. Let this be your first big lesson: the Swordmaster is one of the most powerful things in the game. Start saving early.

walkthrough.title: Why the Swordmaster Matters
walkthrough.text: With two agents, you play two cards for their board space effects and reveal three cards for combat and persuasion. With three agents, you play three cards for board effects and reveal two. That extra board space action every round compounds over the whole game. Players who unlock the Swordmaster early almost always have a significant advantage. Make it a priority to gather eight solari in the first few rounds.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Second Agent Turn
walkthrough.text: Player one has one agent left. They play a card with a Bene Gesserit icon and send their second agent to the Selective Breeding space. This gives one influence with the Bene Gesserit and lets them trash a card from their hand or discard pile. Player one trashes one of their weak starting cards. This is deck thinning, and it is incredibly important.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Keeping a Bloated Deck
walkthrough.text: Player two has been buying new cards from the Imperium Row but has not trashed any weak starting cards. Their deck now has thirteen cards instead of ten. That means their powerful new cards come up less often, diluted by weak starters. Deck quality matters more than deck size. Always look for opportunities to trash the weakest cards in your deck, especially those starting cards that give only one persuasion and nothing else.

walkthrough.title: Player Two and Three: Reveal Turns
walkthrough.text: Players two and three have now used both their agents. They each take their Reveal Turn. They flip their remaining hand cards face up and add up the combat strength. Player two has three sword icons across their revealed cards. Player three has two sword icons plus one troop in the conflict area for two more combat strength, giving them a total of four. They each move their combat marker on the combat track.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Reveal Turn
walkthrough.text: Player one flips their remaining cards. They have two sword icons from revealed cards plus one troop deployed earlier for two more, totaling four combat strength. Player one also adds up their persuasion icons. They have four persuasion total. Player one spends four persuasion to buy a new card from the Imperium Row that has strong combat icons. Good investment for future rounds.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Fighting Every Battle
walkthrough.text: Player three committed heavily to combat this round, deploying multiple troops. But look at the conflict card — first place only awards one victory point. Meanwhile, Player three burned a water token and a good card just to deploy troops. Sometimes the conflict reward is not worth the resources you spend fighting for it. Smart players pick their battles. If the reward is small, save your troops and resources for a bigger conflict next round.

walkthrough.title: Combat Resolution
walkthrough.text: Time for combat. Player one has four combat strength. Player two has three. Player three has four. Players one and three are tied. The tiebreaker goes to whoever is closer to the first-player marker going clockwise. Player one wins first place and claims one victory point and two spice. Player three gets second place and gains one troop. Player two gets third place with nothing. Combat intrigue cards could have changed everything here, but nobody played one this round.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Forgetting Alliance Tokens
walkthrough.text: Player two gained one Emperor influence this round and is now at level one. They need to remember that reaching level two earns the alliance token worth one victory point. But Player two has been ignoring all the other faction tracks entirely. Alliance tokens are a reliable source of victory points that you should be working toward every game. Spread your visits across at least two or three factions.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Undervaluing Spice
walkthrough.text: Player two has been collecting solari aggressively but completely ignoring spice. Spice is the premium resource because many of the strongest Imperium cards require spice, certain board spaces cost spice, and spice can be converted to other resources at excellent rates. Solari is useful, but spice opens doors that solari cannot. Make sure you are visiting spice locations regularly.

walkthrough.title: Recall Phase
walkthrough.text: The round is over. Everyone picks up their agents from the board. Troops in the conflict area return to each player's garrison. All played and revealed cards go to the discard pile. Each player draws five new cards from their deck. If your deck runs out, shuffle your discard pile to form a new deck. Refill any gaps in the Imperium Row.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Preview
walkthrough.text: A new conflict card is flipped. This time the first-place reward is two victory points. That is a big prize, and everyone should take combat more seriously this round. Player one should leverage their spice advantage. Player two should push toward the Emperor alliance token. Player three should conserve resources instead of overcommitting. Each round gets more intense as decks improve and factions advance.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have walked through your first round of Dune Imperium. You have seen how Agent Turns and Reveal Turns work, how cards serve dual purposes, how combat resolves, and how faction influence earns victory points. You have also learned from common beginner mistakes: unlock the Swordmaster early, thin your deck, pick your battles, pursue alliance tokens, and do not sleep on spice.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to board spaces, card types, and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. The spice must flow!

bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 example players using different leaders
Covers one complete round: Agent Turns → Reveal Turns → Combat
Follow along regardless of your player count

bullets: New conflict card flipped — rewards escalate each round
Decks improve, factions advance, tension builds
Adjust strategy based on what opponents are pursuing

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Practiced Agent Turns, Reveal Turns, combat, and deck building
Key lessons: unlock Swordmaster, thin deck, pick battles, pursue alliances, value spice
Ready for a full game!

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for spaces and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, let's cover the biggest traps new players fall into. First, ignoring the Swordmaster. That third agent is a game-changer and should be an early priority. Second, fighting every single combat instead of picking your battles. Third, not thinning your starting deck. Those weak starting cards dilute your draws and slow your engine. Fourth, forgetting that alliance tokens are worth victory points. Fifth, treating all resources as equal when spice is clearly the most valuable. Avoid these and you will be ahead of most first-time players.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Dual Nature of Cards
walkthrough.text: This is the single most important concept in Dune Imperium. Every card has two uses, and you can only pick one each round. Use it on an Agent Turn for its board space access, or save it for your Reveal Turn for combat strength and persuasion. New players often play all their cards as agents without thinking about what they are giving up in combat and buying power. Before you play a card, ask yourself: is this card worth more as a board space visit or as combat and persuasion during my reveal? That question will guide better decisions every turn.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize the Swordmaster
walkthrough.text: The Swordmaster costs eight solari and gives you a permanent third agent for the rest of the game. Do the math: if you unlock it in round two or three, you get a bonus action for seven or eight remaining rounds. That is seven extra board space visits over the course of the game. No single card purchase comes close to that value. Start gathering solari from round one. Visit the Wealth space, collect solari from cards, and resist spending it on anything else until you have your Swordmaster.

walkthrough.title: Thin Your Deck Early
walkthrough.text: Your starting deck has ten cards, and most of them are weak. They give one persuasion and nothing else. Every time you draw one of those cards instead of a powerful Imperium card, you have wasted a slot in your five-card hand. Trashing weak cards is like taking a shortcut to your best cards. Look for board spaces like Selective Breeding and Research Station that let you trash cards. Some Imperium cards also have trash abilities. A deck of eight strong cards draws better than a deck of fourteen mixed ones.

walkthrough.title: Pick Your Battles
walkthrough.text: Not every conflict is worth winning. Before committing troops and combat intrigue cards, look at the reward on the conflict card. If first place only gives one troop and some solari, it might not be worth burning your resources. Save your combat strength for rounds where victory points are on the line. Also watch your opponents. If someone has deployed three troops and you have one, the math might not work in your favor. Let them have it and focus on building your engine for a bigger fight next round.

walkthrough.title: Work the Faction Tracks
walkthrough.text: Faction influence is one of the most reliable paths to victory points. Each track has an alliance token at level two worth one victory point and a bonus victory point at level four. Reaching level two in two different factions is two victory points for relatively low effort. But be careful: alliance tokens at level two can be stolen if another player reaches that level. Push to level four when you can, because that bonus is permanent. Also, higher influence levels unlock better board spaces, so faction progress has both immediate and long-term payoff.

walkthrough.title: Respect the Spice
walkthrough.text: Spice is the premium resource in Dune Imperium, just like in the Dune universe. It is harder to come by than solari and more versatile than water. Many of the best cards in the Imperium deck require spice or reward you for spending it. Desert locations that accumulate bonus spice can give you three or four spice in a single visit if you time it right. Think of solari as your everyday currency and spice as your strategic asset. If you see spice accumulating on the board, plan your next round to grab it before an opponent does.

walkthrough.title: Balance Short and Long Term
walkthrough.text: Dune Imperium rewards players who balance immediate gains with long-term investment. Buying cards and thinning your deck is a long-term play that pays off in later rounds. Winning combat is often an immediate gain. Faction influence is somewhere in between, giving you both alliance tokens now and better spaces later. The best strategy is to invest in your deck and Swordmaster early, then pivot to aggressive combat and faction pushes in the middle rounds, and close out with endgame intrigue cards and decisive conflict victories.

bullets: Ignoring the Swordmaster — 3rd agent is a must-buy
Fighting every combat — pick battles worth the resources
Not thinning the deck — weak starting cards dilute draws
Forgetting alliance tokens — free VP at faction level 2
Treating spice like any other resource — it's premium

Cannot use both — always a tradeoff

New players over-play cards as agents, leaving weak reveals

summary.title: Respect the Spice

Best Imperium cards require or reward spice
Accumulated bonus spice on board = high-value targets

summary.title: Balance Short and Long Term
bullets: Early: invest in deck + Swordmaster (long-term)
Mid: push factions + selective combat (medium-term)
Late: endgame intrigue cards + decisive conflicts (short-term)
Best players balance all three throughout

walkthrough.title: Swordmaster Rush
walkthrough.text: The Swordmaster Rush is one of the most powerful openings in Dune Imperium. The goal is to unlock your third agent as fast as possible, ideally in round one or two. Focus entirely on solari generation in your first few turns. Visit the Wealth space, play cards that generate solari, and ignore almost everything else. Eight solari sounds like a lot, but with focused play and maybe a plot intrigue card, you can get there fast. Once you have three agents, every subsequent round you have a massive action advantage over players still working with two.

walkthrough.title: Spice Hoarding
walkthrough.text: Spice accumulates on desert board spaces when they go uncollected. A patient player can wait until three or four spice tokens pile up on The Great Flat or Hagga Basin, then swoop in for a massive haul. The trick is timing. If you grab spice too early, you get less. If you wait too long, an opponent beats you to it. Pair spice collection with cards that give extra spice bonuses. Use your spice to buy the most powerful Imperium cards like The Spice Must Flow, which gives you a permanent victory point. Spice is the gateway to the strongest plays in the game.

walkthrough.title: Faction Dominance
walkthrough.text: Instead of spreading thin across all four factions, pick two and push them hard. Getting to level four in a faction gives you a permanent victory point that nobody can take away, plus access to the most powerful board spaces for that faction. The Emperor track rewards solari-heavy strategies with military might. The Spacing Guild rewards economic play. The Bene Gesserit rewards card manipulation and intrigue. The Fremen reward desert and spice strategies. Choose factions that synergize with your leader's ability and the cards you are buying.

walkthrough.title: Combat Timing
walkthrough.text: The conflict cards escalate as the game progresses. Conflict one cards offer modest rewards. Conflict two cards start offering victory points. Conflict three cards offer the biggest prizes. Smart players conserve combat resources during the early rounds and explode in the late game when victories are worth the most. Stockpile combat intrigue cards in rounds one and two. Build up your troop count in the garrison. Then when a Conflict three card offers two victory points for first place, you go all in with troops, swords, and intrigue cards to guarantee the win.

walkthrough.title: Deck Sculpting
walkthrough.text: Advanced deck building goes beyond just buying good cards. You want to sculpt your deck so that every five-card hand is strong. This means aggressively trashing weak starting cards while selectively buying cards that serve multiple purposes. Look for cards with good agent icons and strong reveal values. Avoid cards that are great in one mode but useless in the other. The perfect deck has eight to ten cards where every possible hand of five gives you meaningful Agent Turns and a strong Reveal Turn. Lean decks win games.

walkthrough.title: Intrigue Card Management
walkthrough.text: Intrigue cards are your secret weapons, and managing them well separates good players from great ones. Combat intrigue cards are most valuable in the late game when conflict rewards are biggest. Plot intrigue cards should be played as soon as their conditions are met. Endgame intrigue cards sit in your hand until final scoring, so grab them when you can fulfill their conditions. Never burn a combat intrigue card on a small conflict. Hoard them for the decisive battles. And always count your opponents' intrigue cards. If someone has four or five intrigue cards and seems relaxed about combat, they probably have a devastating surprise planned.

walkthrough.title: The Water Economy
walkthrough.text: Water is the most constrained resource in Dune Imperium. You only start with one, and there are limited ways to get more. But water unlocks the most lucrative desert spaces. The Great Flat and Hagga Basin both require water but give massive spice payouts. Think of water as a key that opens the best spaces on the board. Never waste water on low-value plays. Save it for turns when accumulated spice on desert spaces is at its peak. Some cards generate water as a bonus — these are extremely valuable and should be prioritized during deck building.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Acceleration
walkthrough.text: The endgame in Dune Imperium can happen suddenly. A player at seven or eight victory points can jump to ten in a single round by winning a big conflict and scoring an endgame intrigue card. Watch the victory point track closely starting around round five or six. If you are in the lead, try to end the game quickly by pushing for the final victory points. If you are behind, you need to block the leader. Contest the same combats they are fighting. Claim alliance tokens they need. The Spice Must Flow card from the Reserve gives one immediate victory point and is often the card that closes out a game. At nine solari worth of persuasion, it is expensive but game-ending.

entries.definition: A turn where you play one card from your hand and place one agent on a matching board space. You gain the board space's benefits plus the card's agent-turn bonuses. You must take all Agent Turns before your Reveal Turn.

A turn where you flip all remaining hand cards face up. You add their combat strength to your total and spend their persuasion to buy new cards. Each player takes exactly one Reveal Turn per round, after all their Agent Turns.
A worker meeple placed on board spaces during Agent Turns. Each player starts with two agents and can unlock a third (the Swordmaster). The Mentat can be hired as a temporary additional agent.

A third agent that can be unlocked for eight Solari. Once purchased, the Swordmaster is a permanent agent available every round for the rest of the game. One of the most powerful investments available.

A special token on the board that any player can hire for two Solari during their turn. The Mentat acts as an additional agent for that round only, then returns to the board. Also draws you a card when hired.

The premium resource in the game, represented by orange crystal tokens. Gained primarily from desert board spaces. Used for powerful card effects, board space costs, and high-value Imperium cards. Also serves as the tiebreaker for final scoring.
The gold coin currency used for everyday purchases. Needed to hire the Mentat (two Solari) and unlock the Swordmaster (eight Solari). Also used at certain board spaces and for card effects.
The most constrained resource, represented by blue drop tokens. Required to access the most lucrative desert board spaces like The Great Flat and Hagga Basin. Harder to acquire than Spice or Solari.

Cube-shaped pieces in each player's color. Start in the garrison and can be deployed to the conflict area via board spaces and card effects. Each troop in the conflict area adds two combat strength. Surviving troops return to the garrison after combat.
The area on the board where a player's unused troops are stored. Troops deploy from the garrison to the conflict area and return to the garrison after combat resolves.
The zone on the board where deployed troops are placed to participate in combat. Each troop here adds two combat strength. After combat, all troops return to their owner's garrison.

Your total fighting power in a round's combat. Calculated by adding sword icons from revealed cards plus two per troop in the conflict area, plus any combat intrigue card bonuses. Tracked on the combat track.
A numbered track on the board where players mark their total combat strength each round. Used to determine ranking for conflict rewards. Reset to zero each round during recall.

A card flipped at the start of each round showing rewards for first, second, and third place in combat. Conflict cards escalate across three tiers (I, II, III), with later cards offering better rewards including victory points.
The first set of conflict cards in the deck. Offers modest rewards such as resources and troops. Represents the early skirmishes before the real war begins.
The middle set of conflict cards. Begins offering victory points as rewards. Represents the escalation of the conflict for control of Arrakis.
The final and most rewarding conflict cards. Offers the biggest prizes, often including two victory points for first place. Running out of this deck can also end the game.
The buying power generated by revealed cards during your Reveal Turn. Spend persuasion to acquire new cards from the Imperium Row or the Reserve. Persuasion does not carry over between rounds.
The market of five face-up cards available for purchase with persuasion. Refilled from the Imperium deck whenever a card is bought. Cards vary in cost and power.
The large shuffled deck of cards that refills the Imperium Row. Contains a wide variety of cards with different agent icons, combat strength, persuasion values, and special abilities.

Two always-available card piles: Arrakis Liaison (costs two persuasion, basic utility) and The Spice Must Flow (costs nine persuasion, worth one victory point). Unlike the Imperium Row, these never run out.
A Reserve card costing two persuasion. A basic card useful for early deck building when the Imperium Row does not have affordable options. Provides flexible agent icons.

A Reserve card costing nine persuasion that is worth one victory point. One of the most reliable late-game victory point sources. Often the card that pushes a player to ten victory points for the win.
Permanently remove a card from your deck. Trashed cards go to a common trash pile and cannot be recovered. Trashing weak starting cards is essential for improving deck quality and draw consistency.

Special cards drawn from the intrigue deck via board spaces and card effects. Kept secret in hand until played. Come in three types: Combat, Plot, and Endgame. Powerful surprises that can swing the game.
A type of intrigue card played during the combat phase. Adds extra combat strength or provides other combat-related bonuses. Most valuable during late-game conflicts with big rewards.

A type of intrigue card that can be played during your Agent Turn or Reveal Turn. Provides immediate bonuses like extra resources, troops, or influence. Should be played as soon as conditions are met.

A type of intrigue card that is scored at the end of the game for bonus victory points if specific conditions are met. Held in hand throughout the game and never played — just revealed during final scoring.

One of the four faction influence tracks. The Emperor faction is associated with Solari generation and military power. Board spaces aligned with the Emperor often grant Solari, troops, or combat bonuses.

One of the four faction influence tracks. The Spacing Guild controls interstellar travel and commerce. Board spaces aligned with the Guild often grant economic benefits, spice trade opportunities, and card manipulation.

One of the four faction influence tracks. The Bene Gesserit are a secretive sisterhood with mental and physical training. Board spaces aligned with the Bene Gesserit often grant intrigue cards, card draw, and deck manipulation including trashing.

One of the four faction influence tracks. The Fremen are the native people of Arrakis. Board spaces aligned with the Fremen involve desert travel, water management, and troop deployment. Higher Fremen influence unlocks powerful desert bonuses.

A measure of your relationship with each of the four factions, tracked on influence tracks running from level zero to level four. Gained by sending agents to faction-aligned board spaces. Higher levels unlock alliance tokens, bonus victory points, and powerful board spaces.

A token on each faction track at level two, worth one victory point. When you reach level two, you claim it. If another player also reaches level two on the same track, they steal it from you. Push to level four for a permanent bonus victory point that cannot be stolen.

The winning condition of the game. First player to reach ten victory points triggers the game end. Gained from conflict rewards, alliance tokens, faction level four bonuses, certain cards like The Spice Must Flow, and endgame intrigue cards. Tracked on the VP track around the board edge.

Each player's unique character card chosen during setup. Leaders have a special ability active for the entire game and a signet ring icon that enhances certain Imperium cards. Leaders include characters like Duke Leto Atreides, Baron Harkonnen, and others from the Dune universe.

An icon on each leader card. Some Imperium cards have a signet ring symbol that grants a bonus effect when played by a player whose leader matches. Adds thematic flavor and strategic variation between leaders.

The identical ten-card deck each player begins with. Contains basic cards with limited agent icons, weak persuasion, and minimal combat strength. Designed to be improved through buying and trashing over the course of the game.

A phase at the end of each round. If any desert board spaces with the maker icon were occupied this round, bonus spice tokens are placed on designated board spaces. This creates accumulating spice incentives for future rounds.

The final phase of each round. All agents return to their owners. Troops in the conflict area return to garrisons. Played and revealed cards are discarded. Each player draws five new cards. The Imperium Row is refilled.
A city board space that grants one troop deployment and one card draw. One of the most popular early-game destinations for building combat presence while improving your hand.
A city board space that grants one troop deployment and one intrigue card. Similar to Arrakeen but gives an intrigue card instead of a card draw. Both city spaces are high-priority targets.
A desert board space that costs one water and grants two spice plus any accumulated bonus spice. One of the highest-value spaces on the board when bonus spice has piled up over multiple rounds.
A desert board space that costs one water and grants one spice plus any accumulated bonus spice. Slightly less rewarding than The Great Flat but still a strong spice collection option.
An Emperor-aligned board space that grants two Solari and one Emperor influence. A key destination for players pursuing the Swordmaster or building Solari reserves.

A Bene Gesserit-aligned board space that grants one Bene Gesserit influence and lets you trash a card from your hand or discard pile. Essential for deck thinning and one of the most strategically important spaces.
A board space that grants one victory point and a seat on the High Council for the rest of the game, providing a permanent bonus. Extremely competitive — only one player can claim it per game.
